skillName: Use formal methods for addition of integers
misconceptionSummary: Students misalign columns when numbers have different numbers of digits.

steps.narration: The student lined up the left edges, so the eight in eighty-seven landed in the thousands column and the seven in the hundreds. That gives the wrong answer because the digits are in the wrong columns.

visuals.ruleText: Always align the ones digits — then every other column lines up automatically
visuals.subText: Shorter numbers get leading spaces, not leading zeros in the calculation

steps.narration: Align from the right — ones under ones. The shorter number, eighty-seven, has its seven under the two and its eight under the zero. Thousands and hundreds of the shorter number are simply empty.
Aligned correctly: two plus seven is nine, zero plus eight is eight, three plus nothing is three, four plus nothing is four. The answer is four thousand three hundred and eighty-nine.

visuals.label: Align ones on the right — shorter number gets leading spaces

steps.narration: Four thousand three hundred and eighty-nine. Right-align the digits and every column takes care of itself.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: aligning numbers by their left edges. Always align the ones column on the right — shorter numbers have empty spaces on the left, not at the right.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Line up digits from the right, not the left. The ones column must sit directly above the ones column. Empty positions on the left stay blank.
